title: How to Find Accurate Prayer Times: A Complete Guide for Muslims
excerpt: Salah is the second pillar of Islam, and knowing its exact times is obligatory. This comprehensive guide explains how prayer times are calculated, what affects them, and how to find yours instantly.
author: QuranBookk
content:
Knowing when to pray is not optional — it is inseparable from the prayer itself. The Quran states clearly:

<AyahBlock
  arabic="إِنَّ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ كَانَتْ عَلَى ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ كِتَٰبًا مَّوْقُوتًا"
  translation="Indeed, prayer has been decreed upon the believers a decree of specified times."
  reference="Surah An-Nisa 4:103"
/>

This verse makes clear that the *time* of prayer is part of the obligation. Praying outside its window without a legitimate excuse is a serious matter. So how are these times actually determined?

## How Prayer Times Are Calculated

Prayer times are derived from the position of the sun relative to the horizon. They are not fixed to a clock — they shift every day of the year and differ by geographic location.

**Fajr** begins at true astronomical dawn — when a white light spreads along the horizon, roughly 1–1.5 hours before sunrise depending on your latitude and the calculation method used.

**Dhuhr** enters once the sun crosses its zenith (highest point). This is true solar noon, not 12:00 PM on your clock.

**Asr** is the most debated. The majority position (Shafi'i, Maliki, Hanbali) says Asr enters when an object's shadow equals its own length plus the shadow at Dhuhr. The Hanafi school says it enters when the shadow is *twice* the object's length. This can result in a difference of 45–60 minutes in some locations.

**Maghrib** enters immediately after sunset — the moment the sun's upper disc disappears below the horizon.

**Isha** enters when the red twilight disappears from the sky (roughly 60–90 minutes after Maghrib). In high latitudes during summer, this can become a special challenge.

## Why Do Prayer Times Differ Between Apps?

If you have ever compared two prayer time apps and found different results, the reason lies in the *calculation method*. The main global standards are:

| Organisation | Used by |
|---|---|
| Muslim World League (MWL) | Europe, Far East, parts of Americas |
| Islamic Society of North America (ISNA) | USA, Canada |
| Egyptian General Authority | Egypt, many Arab countries |
| Umm al-Qura | Saudi Arabia |
| University of Islamic Sciences Karachi | Pakistan, Afghanistan, India, Bangladesh |

Each organisation defines Fajr and Isha using slightly different angles below the horizon (15°, 18°, 19.5° etc.), leading to meaningfully different results.

## The Challenge of High Latitudes

Muslims living in northern Europe, Canada, or Alaska face a unique challenge. During summer months, the sun barely sets — sometimes not at all. In these cases, scholars recommend following either:

- **Nearest city method** — follow the times of the nearest city where twilight disappears normally
- **Fixed hours method** — assign fixed durations (e.g. Fajr = 1.5 hours before sunrise, Isha = 1.5 hours after sunset)

Most scholars from countries like Norway, Finland, and northern Canada have issued fatwas recommending one of these methods for their communities.

## Sunnahs Related to Prayer Times

The Prophet ﷺ said: *"The best deed is prayer at its proper time."* (Sahih al-Bukhari)

Some sunnahs regarding prayer timing:
- Praying Fajr in its early time is among the most beloved deeds to Allah
- Dhuhr in summer should ideally be delayed slightly (ibrad) — praying after the intense midday heat subsides
- Asr should not be delayed until the sun yellows
- Maghrib should be prayed promptly without delay
- Isha can be delayed up to the first third of the night, which is recommended

Understanding and observing these times is not just a legal requirement — it is a profound way of structuring your entire day around your Creator.
